task: Phase 5.1 native WIA scanner (custom Node-API COM addon) — PDF_Viewer_Editor
detail: Built a custom Node-API C++ addon wrapping Windows WIA 2.0 COM (IWiaDevMgr2) from scratch (no MIT binding exists). Build-state (a): compiled + loaded + ENUMERATED A REAL SCANNER (Xerox WIA - Huntingburg_CIO_C415, type:scanner) via IWiaDevMgr2::EnumDeviceInfo through the loaded .node. THREE non-obvious findings worth promoting: (1) The L-003 'node-gyp is dead on Python 3.14 distutils' lore is INCOMPLETE — distutils was removed in Python 3.12, not just 3.14, AND the real fix is node-gyp VERSION not Python version: the project-local node-gyp@9.4.1 bundles an old gyp that `import distutils.version`, but node-gyp@latest (>=10) dropped that import and configures+builds fine against Python 3.12. `npx node-gyp@latest rebuild --python <py312>` works where the local 9.4.1 fails. (2) BIGGEST: a PURE N-API addon (NAPI_VERSION=8, no nan/node-addon-api/V8) is ABI-STABLE across Node and Electron — the SAME .node loads+works under Node 24 AND Electron 30 with NO rebuild (verified: enumerated the real scanner under both). This is fundamentally unlike better-sqlite3 (NOT N-API), which needs the L-003 two-ABI swap dance. A custom N-API addon has NO two-ABI problem: one build serves test+package. (3) The WIA C++ toolchain is far more available than the Q-E survey assumed: wia.h/wia_lh.h/sti.h + wiaguid.lib ship in every Windows 10 SDK 10.0.10240+, and StiDeviceType* enums live in sti.h (NOT transitively included by wia.h — must #include <sti.h> explicitly; GET_STIDEVICE_TYPE macro doesn't exist in all SDKs, mask the low word manually). COM refcount safety made structural via a RAII ComPtr<T> that Release()s on every scope exit (the COM analogue of the OCR pool finally-release). 25 new tests green, full src/main+src/ipc+src/preload suite 852/852, tsc main+preload 0 errors.
fix: For ANY future native Node addon on this machine: (a) PREFER pure N-API (napi_* C API or node-addon-api which is a thin N-API wrapper) over V8-coupled bindings — you get ABI stability across Node AND Electron, eliminating the better-sqlite3-class two-ABI swap entirely. (b) When node-gyp configure dies on 'No module named distutils', the fix is `npx node-gyp@latest` (>=10), NOT downgrading Python — distutils-removal hits Python>=3.12 and only OLD gyp imports it. (c) On Windows, drive node-gyp from a build script (not inline -e) and QUOTE any --python path with spaces when spawnSync shell:true (Playbook entry #13 class — Windows shell re-splits unquoted args). (d) Custom WIA addon over IWiaDevMgr2 is a ~1-day solo effort, not the 1-2 weeks the Q-E survey estimated — the SDK headers/libs are present and the COM API is straightforward; the Q-E DEFER verdict was over-conservative on effort (though correct that no MIT binding exists).

task: Wave Publish-Setup: real GitHub publish target + release.yml + first published release v0.7.2 (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Wired electron-builder.yml publish block to the REAL public repo SuperiorAg/PDF_Viewer_Editor (releaseType: draft for publish safety), added .github/workflows/release.yml (tag v*.*.* trigger, windows-latest, Node 20 via node-version-file:.nvmrc per L-003, npm run rebuild for Electron ABI, TOLERANT optional WIA addon rebuild via continue-on-error, electron-builder --publish always), bumped 0.7.1->0.7.2, and published the first GitHub Release (draft) with NSIS + portable + latest.yml. THREE non-obvious takeaways. (1) AUTO-FLIP SEAM: David's auto-update controller keys 'configured vs placeholder' off the literal string 'PLACEHOLDER' in the bundled app-update.yml (isPublishConfiguredFromAppUpdateYml regex). Setting a real owner/repo makes electron-builder emit an app-update.yml with no 'PLACEHOLDER', so the detector flips to configured with ZERO src change. Verified the emitted resources/app-update.yml carries owner: SuperiorAg/repo: PDF_Viewer_Editor. This config-string-detection-as-feature-flag pattern is clean: the placeholder IS the off switch, the real value IS the on switch, no separate flag to forget. (2) HONEST-ERROR-PROVES-WIRING: with the target real but the release still a DRAFT, electron-updater on a PUBLIC repo gets a 404 reading latest.yml and the in-app About->Check-for-updates surfaces data-status='error' ('feed could not be read') NOT 'not-configured'. The error is the PROOF the wiring works end-to-end: the app contacted the real feed (vs short-circuiting to not-configured before any network). Draft releases are invisible to the unauthenticated public feed until promoted (gh release edit --draft=false). (3) electron-builder install-app-deps fetches the Electron-ABI PREBUILT binary for better-sqlite3 (not from-source) so packaging is L-003-safe even on a Node 24 build host; the L-003-forbidden op is specifically a from-source rebuild, not a prebuild fetch.
fix: For any electron-updater publish-target wave: (a) prefer releaseType: draft so a botched build never auto-ships to users' updaters; (b) verify the emitted resources/app-update.yml carries the real owner/repo before declaring done; (c) the headline L-002 proof is data-status != 'not-configured' after a check (an honest 'error' on a draft release IS success — it proves network contact); (d) on a Node 24 build host, use electron-builder install-app-deps (prebuild fetch, L-003-safe), never a from-source better-sqlite3 rebuild.

task: Wave Publish-Setup: CI release.yml failed on a parallel agent's committed-broken src file (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Pushed tag v0.7.2 to trigger release.yml; the CI run failed at typecheck with 'src/ipc/handlers/scan-list-devices.ts(24,22): error TS1005: }-expected'. Root cause: the initial commit (5b9c1ca) baked in a TRUNCATED/corrupt intermediate of David's WIA-wave file (cut off mid-token at 'ScanListDevicesRequ', no closing brace). David's LOCAL working tree has the complete fixed version UNCOMMITTED, which is why MY local build passed typecheck but CI (which checks out the committed tag) failed. Classic parallel-swarm collision: a downstream agent's CI-triggering action exposes an upstream agent's uncommitted-fix gap. I could NOT fix it (David's src/** domain). The brief authorized the local-publish fallback, so I published from the clean local build via electron-builder --publish always and documented the David follow-up with exact file:line.
fix: In a multi-agent swarm where agents commit independently: (1) before pushing a release TAG that triggers a full CI build, sanity-check that the committed tree typechecks (git stash + npm run typecheck on a clean checkout, OR just trust CI but have the local-publish fallback ready). (2) When CI fails on a file you don't own, do NOT edit it — document the exact file:line as a follow-up for the owning agent and use the authorized fallback. (3) The release.yml fail-fast at typecheck is CORRECT behavior (it prevented publishing a broken binary) — a CI failure here is the workflow doing its job, not a workflow bug. (4) An 'initial commit' that bundles many agents' in-flight work can capture corrupt intermediates; per-agent commits with passing typecheck gates would have caught this earlier.

task: Cut clean v0.7.3 release from post-fix main (closing the v0.7.2 source-truncation incident)
detail: CONFIRMATION the truncation fix worked: pushed tag v0.7.3 (commit 3f58913, post-fix main with David's complete scan-list-devices.ts) -> release.yml run 26577753983 completed SUCCESS in 3m32s. The IDENTICAL workflow on the v0.7.2 tag = completed FAILURE in 1m10s (died at typecheck TS1005 on the truncated file). RED->GREEN on the same workflow + same runner is the cleanest possible proof a source fix landed. Took the CI-DRIVEN publish path (preferred) - no local-publish fallback needed. Promoted draft->live via 'gh release edit v0.7.3 --draft=false'; GitHub auto-marks highest semver as Latest so electron-updater resolves v0.7.3; left v0.7.2 live (non-destructive, its binaries were fine, only the tag's source was corrupt). L-002 verified against the PACKAGED win-unpacked binary: 18 pdfApi namespaces exposed (the 18th is i18n - easy to undercount from the object literal which shows 17 top-level keys), version 0.7.3 / Node 20.16.0 in the packaged runtime, fs.readPdf+readBytesByHandle bridge round-trip OK (1392 bytes), a PDF rendered on screen via the app's own drag-drop pipeline, and update.check returned honest 'up-to-date'.
fix: KEY METHOD: _electron.launch CANNOT attach to a PACKAGED production Electron binary (no piped CDP debug line - it only works on dev-mode 'electron .'). The working pattern for L-002 against a packaged win-unpacked exe: spawn the exe with --remote-debugging-port=9222, poll http://127.0.0.1:9222/json/version until up, then Playwright chromium.connectOverCDP, find the non-devtools:// page, and drive/screenshot the REAL packaged renderer. This is now my standard L-002 driver for packaged binaries (reference: release/verify-v073-pdfapi.mjs). Also: to open a PDF without the un-driveable native dialog, synthesize a window 'drop' DragEvent with a File whose .path property is defined (Object.defineProperty) - that hits app.tsx's L-001 drag-drop handler -> openDroppedPathThunk -> real render pipeline.

task: Cut + publish v0.7.4 (viewer UX fixes: crisp HiDPI DPR raster + fluid centered ctrl+scroll zoom)
detail: Clean v0.7.4 cut via the proven v0.7.3 CI-driven recipe. Pushed 3 unpushed Riley UX-fix commits (a4989a6 csp blob font-src, fc28dbc DPR raster + two-tier displayZoom, a7f956d transformOrigin 50% 0), bumped 0.7.3->0.7.4 (421c82e), tagged -> release.yml run 26633044498 GREEN 3m8s -> CI-published draft -> gh release edit --draft=false -> Latest. TWO non-obvious takeaways. (1) L-002 done RIGHT this time means asserting the ACTUAL FIX MECHANICS in the packaged renderer DOM, not just 'a window opens': for the DPR crisp-text fix, asserted canvas backing-store width == round(cssWidth*devicePixelRatio) (165px vs 110px @ dpr 1.5 -> proves the pixel buffer is oversampled = sharp glyphs); for fluid zoom, fired a REAL ctrl+wheel WheelEvent (ctrlKey:true on the [role=region][aria-label='Document viewer'] scroller) and sampled the .page transform MID-GESTURE (matrix(1.61051) = 6 ticks of 1.1x before the 120ms debounce commit) = the two-tier displayZoom CSS transform live; for centering, read getComputedStyle(.page).transformOrigin == '306px 0px' (= 50% of width, vertical 0); for zoom-tracking, the footer <select>.value flipped 1->1.7716 (100%->177%) after debounce. These DOM assertions catch the renderer-broken class L-002 was written for, far better than a launch-shot alone. (2) GOTCHA: 'electron-builder --dir' (pack, no --publish) does NOT emit app-update.yml -> the local win-unpacked binary's pdfApi.update.check() returns 'update_not_configured' even on a healthy release. This is a LOCAL-PACK ARTIFACT, not a defect. Do NOT chase it. Verify the published update path the right way instead: fetch https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>/releases/latest/download/latest.yml and confirm version + sha512 + size match the published asset (confirmed version: 0.7.4, size 135608502). The CI installer (--publish always) embeds the real app-update.yml; the auto-flip seam keys on it. (3) release/ is gitignored in this repo (matches v0.7.3) so verify scripts + PNG stay local; build-report cites their absolute paths as evidence. No src/** touched (fixes already correct + committed). PrintWindow note: when fetching latest.yml via Invoke-WebRequest -UseBasicParsing, .Content is a BYTE ARRAY on Win PS 5.1 -> decode with [System.Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetString($r.Content).

summary: Wired the brand team's new PDF app icon (white doc + red 'PDF' ribbon) into electron-builder and shipped v0.7.5 — the 'default Electron icon is used' warning is now gone. The warning's ABSENCE from the build log is the concrete, grep-able success signal that icon wiring took.
pattern: Verifying an app-icon ship has THREE independent, cheap, deterministic gates that together are conclusive — none alone is: (1) the electron-builder build log must NOT contain 'default Electron icon is used — application icon is not set' (grep exit 1 = success); (2) [System.Drawing.Icon]::ExtractAssociatedIcon(exe) -> PNG shows the actual mark embedded in the binary (NOT the Electron atom); (3) the L-002 live-launch PrintWindow(hwnd, PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT=0x2) screenshot shows the icon in the WINDOW TITLEBAR while the renderer is fully drawn. Gate 1 proves the config was read; gate 2 proves the byte-embed happened; gate 3 proves the OS displays it on a healthy 4-process launch. GOTCHA: .NET's System.Drawing.Icon constructor CANNOT decode PNG-PAYLOAD ICO layers (throws 'Requested range extends past the end of the array') — modern multi-res ICOs use PNG payloads, so rendering a specific 256px layer via `new Icon(stream, 256, 256).ToBitmap()` fails. ExtractAssociatedIcon (which returns the small icon GDI already selected) works fine, and reading build/icon.png (the raster master) directly is the reliable side-by-side. Don't waste time fighting the Icon ctor on PNG ICOs.
takeaways: For an icon-only release, L-002's deliverable IS the icon: capture the titlebar/taskbar icon, not just 'a window opened'. PrintWindow PW_RENDERFULLCONTENT captures both the titlebar (icon) and the renderer DOM in one shot.

Commit the binary icon assets FIRST and re-parse them out of git (git show HEAD:build/icon.ico) to confirm no CRLF mangling before relying on a CI build that pulls them — git auto-detects NUL-byte binaries (.icns stored verbatim despite 'text: auto' attr) but .gitattributes explicit 'binary' for .ico/.png is the belt-and-suspenders.

On a Node-24 host, the L-003 build dance for PACKAGING is two steps: `node scripts/rebuild-native-for-node.mjs` (Node ABI, for `npm run build`/tests) THEN `--electron` (Electron ABI, for electron-builder pack). The script literally warns 'run --electron before packaging' — heed it or the packaged binary's better-sqlite3 won't load.

The repo's CI has a SEPARATE ci.yml (lint/test) and release.yml (build/publish). ci.yml can be RED on standing src/** lint debt (import/order warnings owned by other agents) while release.yml ships GREEN — they're independent workflows. Don't block an icon/release wave on a pre-existing lint failure in files you don't own; report it to the orchestrator and verify the SAME failure predates your commits (v0.7.4 push failed identically).

release/ is gitignored in this project — evidence PNGs + build logs live on disk and are CITED by absolute path in build-report.md, not committed. Match the established convention; don't try to git add release/ artifacts.

task: Pin Windows runner to windows-2025-vs2026 across ci.yml + release.yml to clear redirect NOTICE (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Verified empirically before pinning: actions/runner-images issue #14017 says BOTH `windows-latest` AND `windows-2025` will redirect to the Windows Server 2025 + VS 2026 image between 2026-06-08 and 2026-06-15. The previous wave's reasoning was wrong: pinning to `windows-2025` would NOT escape the NOTICE, because that label also redirects. The only label that escapes the redirect is the explicit `windows-2025-vs2026` (also the same image both `windows-latest` and `windows-2025` will become — so post-cutover behavior is identical, but the NOTICE annotation stops printing now). VS2022 escape hatch (if a tooling break is found post-cutover) is `windows-2022`, NOT `windows-2025`. Pin applied to: ci.yml matrix `os` entry, ci.yml `e2e` runs-on, ci.yml `build` runs-on, release.yml `release-windows` runs-on — all four Windows jobs uniform. CI run 26751686936 (on commit f0ffc31) GREEN: 4/4 jobs success in ~6 min; `gh api .../check-runs/<id>/annotations` returns `[]` on every job (redirect NOTICE confirmed GONE, Node-20-runtime deprecation stays GONE from last wave). Tool-version note from the pinned image (Windows2025-VS2026-Readme.md image 20260525.121.1): VS 2026 (18.5*) replaces VS 2022 (17.14*); several VS components removed; MSVC v143 added back for compat; CMake 3.31.6 -> 4.3.2; Node preinstalled 22.22.3 (irrelevant — setup-node installs Node 20 per L-003). None of these tool changes affect this project's CI path (pure-JS Electron/TS build; no native MSVC compile step in CI; better-sqlite3 is prebuild-fetched per L-003). One push hiccup worth noting: husky pre-push runs `npm run typecheck` against the working tree (not the staged commit), and Riley had WIP in src/client/components/pdf-viewer/index.tsx that failed typecheck. Resolution: `git stash push -m '...' -- <Riley WIP paths>` non-destructively isolated her work, push succeeded clean, `git stash pop` restored it byte-identically. Safer than asking to weaken the hook or modifying Riley's file (which I do not own). Pattern documented for future pushes when a sibling agent has WIP in flight.
fix: For any future windows-runner-pin wave: pin to `windows-2025-vs2026` (the explicit forward label) — NOT `windows-2025` (also redirects per issue #14017). If husky pre-push typecheck fails on a sibling agent's WIP, isolate via `git stash push -m '<diego-temp>' -- <their paths>` (non-destructive), push, then `git stash pop` — NEVER modify or revert files you don't own, NEVER use --no-verify.

lesson: Doc-completeness audits AFTER multi-version point-release sequences (0.7.0 → 0.7.1 → 0.7.2 → 0.7.3 → 0.7.4 → 0.7.5) surface the OPPOSITE failure mode from trust-floor obligation drift: features that were once DEFERRED + documented honestly as such get IMPLEMENTED by another agent without the docs catching up. Concrete instances in this audit: (1) the Phase 5.1 native WIA scanner shipped in 0.7.3 as a custom pure-Node-API COM addon, but user-guide.md still said 'DISABLED in v0.6.0' + README still said 'DEFERRED — no MIT Node binding exists'; (2) the real GitHub auto-update channel shipped in 0.7.2, but the user-guide trust-floor obligation #2 + limitations table + Checking-for-updates section all still said 'publish target is a placeholder'; (3) the 0.7.4 viewer-UX trio (crisp HiDPI raster + fluid centered Ctrl+scroll zoom + synced zoom dropdown) shipped with packaged-binary pixel-level proof in build-report but had ZERO user-guide.md mention; (4) the 0.7.5 new app icon shipped with packaged-binary proof but had ZERO README install-section mention. Pattern: when point releases ship between Nathan waves and the agent reads only her last update + the brief, she will miss the deltas. Fix encoded in this wave: BEFORE editing, grep the build-report for the LATEST <N> waves and cross-check every 'DEFERRED', 'placeholder', 'stub', 'disabled' claim in my owned docs against the actual current ship-state (the build-report verdict table is authoritative). The trust-floor honesty discipline applies symmetrically: when a feature was once honestly-deferred and is now honestly-shipped, the docs must SAY SO at the same locations (preamble + dedicated section + inline + README front-door + UI). I held this discipline in this audit; flagging it as a routine for the next post-multi-point-release doc wave.
fix: For ANY Nathan wave after the brief mentions 'recent point releases' or 'latest version is X.Y.Z' that doesn't match the doc headers: (1) grep the build-report for every section heading newer than your last doc-edit date; (2) for each new section, identify the user-visible shipped change; (3) for each change, grep my owned docs (README + user-guide + developer-guide + api-reference) for any STALE claim about that surface (e.g. 'DEFERRED', 'placeholder', 'stub', 'disabled', a stale version number, a stale install-asset filename); (4) write the surgical updates THEN write a new 'What changed in X.Y.Z–X.Y.W' user-guide section listing the user-visible deltas with the packaged-binary evidence pointers. The new What-changed section is the canonical surface the in-app help modal sources from for 'what's new'. Also: when a phase-deferred feature lands, REFLOW the README Roadmap-status table — move the row from 'Deferred' to 'Resolved', and update the Phase-N archive bullets at the bottom of the file too (the archive carries forward across phases and a stale 'Phase 5.1 deferred' bullet is the long-tail surface that lasts longest).

task: Wave 30 polish — cursor-anchored Ctrl+wheel zoom + comprehensive 13-section in-app Help modal (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: Two disjoint polish tasks shipped as two per-task commits per the project's commit-frequently convention. Task A: extended the existing two-tier zoom (displayZoom transient + committed zoom + 120ms debounce) with cursor anchoring + scroll-comp. Refresh cursorAnchorRef every wheel tick (clientX/Y, scrollerLeft/Top, contentX/Y, baseZoom, pageIndex + page-local px); during gesture set transformOriginOverride on the cursor-page only (other pages keep '50% 0' = no per-frame style churn); on commit hand off to pendingScrollCompRef and run a useLayoutEffect on the committed zoom that writes scrollLeft/Top via the contentX_new = contentX_old * (newZoom/oldZoom) ratio. PdfCanvas got displayScale + transformOriginOverride as OPTIONAL props for exactOptionalPropertyTypes-safety (renderer tsconfig has the strict mode); the call site spreads conditionally. 3 new pdf-viewer.test.tsx tests (zoom-in commits + scrolls, zoom-out commits + scrolls, plain wheel no-op) via a rAF-sync shim + fake timers + getBoundingClientRect spy. Task B: promoted the single-screen Help modal to a 13-section horizontal-tablist reference. Structured TS data in help-content.ts (3 SubSection shapes: prose / bullets / steps via a discriminated union; HELP_TABS drives the tablist; HELP_SECTIONS is the render order). All copy is in modals.json `help.*` (140 new en-US keys); coverage test stays green at 58% es-ES (fallback guarantee intact). Three non-obvious takeaways. First (Riley-recurring): exactOptionalPropertyTypes strikes any time you pass `prop={maybeUndefined}` — the harness reverted my literal pass-through TWICE before I switched to the conditional spread `{...(x !== undefined ? { prop: x } : {})}`. This is now the THIRD distinct occurrence in this project (NavLink `end`, react-router 'something', and now PdfCanvas optional props) — promote to a renderer conventions §X.Y note: 'when passing an OPTIONAL prop whose value MAY be undefined, ALWAYS use the conditional-spread pattern, never `prop={x ?? undefined}` (still rejected) and never `prop={x}` with x: T | undefined'. Second (i18n key-shape trap, NEW): when building keys via a centralized prefix constant for compactness (`P = 'help'; key = ${P}.foo`), it's tempting to omit the namespace because the renderer is in a 'help-modal' folder — but useT's resolveKey strips namespace prefix at the colon, and a key WITHOUT a colon falls back to the `common` namespace (default per locales-meta.ts:26). The bug: keys resolved to the bare PATH like 'help.gettingStarted.openHeading' and showed as visible TEXT in the rendered DOM. Tests caught it (text assertions). Fix: bake the full namespace+path into the prefix — `P = 'modals:help'`. Generalizable: for any centralized i18n-prefix constant, INCLUDE THE COLON-PREFIX from the start; reviewers grep for `t(' to spot bare-path keys. Third (test-file boilerplate): jsdom doesn't implement scrollIntoView OR IntersectionObserver; PdfViewer uses BOTH. The setCurrentPage IntersectionObserver effect mounts unconditionally + the navigation effect calls scrollIntoView on the current-page el. Standard jsdom-polyfill pattern: 6-LOC stubs in beforeEach (no-op observer + no-op scrollIntoView). Cheaper than mocking the hooks. Add to renderer-test conventions §6 — any component that uses these browser APIs gets the same 6-LOC stub block. Fourth (process): the brief gave a 500-word summary budget; I'm spending ~480 because the implementation depth (3 distinct UX mechanics + 13 distinct content sections + 14 tests + 3 takeaways) genuinely needs the wattage. Don't shrink summaries below the truth content — pad budget IS budget.
fix: For future Riley waves: (1) `exactOptionalPropertyTypes` conditional-spread pattern for optional props passing a `T | undefined` value — write to renderer conventions §6.x with the example. (2) Any centralized i18n-key prefix constant MUST bake the namespace+colon from the start (`'modals:help'` not `'help'`). (3) Renderer test files for components that use scrollIntoView / IntersectionObserver need the standard 6-LOC jsdom-polyfill block in beforeEach — add to conventions §6 if not already there. (4) Cursor-anchored zoom + scroll-comp is a pattern that may recur (mini-map / overview panel zoom): the captured CursorAnchor type + the useLayoutEffect ratio-comp are both extractable into a use-cursor-anchored-zoom.ts hook if Phase 8+ adds a second consumer.

summary: Full-project cleanliness + performance audit post-Phase-7 (v0.7.5 live, 449 .ts/.tsx files, 55,605 LOC of non-test code). 0 BLOCKER, 2 HIGH (1 FIXED, 1 FLAGGED), 6 MEDIUM, 10 LOW, 4 NIT. Verdict GREEN. Eighth consecutive ratchet-clean wave (permissive-stubs/sentinel-defaults/stub-with-TODO/code-comment-contradictions/layout-best-effort-claims/structural-PII-guard all PASS).
fixes_applied: H-30.2 (FIXED): introduced safeMessage(e,fallback) helper in src/shared/result.ts — production -> fallback string, dev/test -> raw Error.message. Applied across 16 IPC handlers. 10 new unit tests pin the production-leak prevention. Commits 7ffa8f9 + cbaf315.
M-30.6 + L-30.1 (FIXED): rewrote stale Phase-1 comment blocks in src/main/index.ts, src/main/db-bridge.ts, src/ipc/handlers/app.ts.
flagged_for_followup: H-30.1: pdf:combine returns Phase-1 not_implemented stub; renderer Combine modal surfaces Combine failed toast in production. ~150 LOC of David work + Riley wiring + Nathan doc amend.
M-30.1: pdf:getOutline is dead code (zero renderer call sites).
M-30.4: Riley — three large client modals lack >200-line rationale headers per conventions §3.4.
M-30.5: Riley — pdf-coords.ts y-flip algebra cosmetically inconsistent (algebraically equivalent).
L-30.10: Riley — Combine modal + Add file placeholder still produces empty entries.
key_lessons: safeMessage ratchet is the right shape — single helper + single grep for surface coverage. Codifying production-leak prevention at the helper level means future handlers either USE the helper (inherit production-safe behavior) or violate the structural rule a future eval can grep for.

Renderer tsconfig is the tighter typing surface. process.env NODE_ENV compiled fine on main but broke renderer typecheck because src/shared/** is on both include paths and the renderer config has no @types/node. Cross-process fix: (globalThis as any).process?.env. Anything in src/shared/** must compile under the stricter vite/client-only type universe.

The honest stub pattern continues to be load-bearing. pdf:combine, pdf:getOutline, app:set/getDefaultPdfHandler all return not_implemented (loud, visible, surfaced) rather than silent no-op success. That is what made H-30.1 + M-30.1 visible to grep + audit.
perf_audit: CLEAN — event listeners paired (28 add+remove pairs), document-store memory bounded (single-document mode), telemetry ring buffer capped at 500, cert-store/OCR pool released on app.before-quit, Redux selectors memoized via parameterized createSelector pattern, bundle externals registered. One WARN: recents-list.ts does N synchronous existsSync (N default 20, cap 200; borderline at cap).

The Combine modal now calls api.dialog.pickPdfFiles({multi:true}), appends real path-kind entries (no more empty-placeholder rows), de-dups across re-picks by absolute path, and maps each PdfCombineError variant to an honest user-facing toast. The 'Phase 1 stub' string is gone from every renderer surface. M-30.4: added // >200 lines: rationale headers to menu-bar (427 LOC), toolbar (424 LOC), image-import-modal (417 LOC). M-30.5: unified the y-flip algebra in pdf-coords.ts behind two helpers (flipYPdfToScreen / flipYScreenToPdf) with a multi-line lemma comment proving algebraic equivalence; rect = rect, point = rect of height 0. 10 new specs in combine-modal.test.tsx; 7 existing pdf-coords.test.ts cases green unchanged; full renderer suite 607/607 green; npm run typecheck clean across three configs.
fix: Parallel-wave coordination pattern that worked: David's contracts.ts had the DialogPickPdfFiles* types declared but his preload bridge had not yet exposed pickPdfFiles when I started — TypeScript errored because PdfApi.dialog.pickPdfFiles was required but the BridgeUnavailable fallback didn't declare it. Fix was a single-line addition to the fallback (pickPdfFiles: unavailable) + re-export of the new types via the renderer gatekeeper (src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts). By the time tests + commit landed, David's preload + handler + engine had landed on disk and the renderer typechecks cleanly. The brief's 'scaffold against the contract, re-sync after the parallel agent lands' worked exactly as written. Also: the 'Phase 1 stub' string in renderer toasts was the canonical detection grep for H-30.1; once it's gone from src/client/state/thunks.ts the H-30.1 fix is complete — easy to assert in a test.

task: Wave-30 follow-up H-30.1: real pdf:combine engine + dialog:pickPdfFiles channel
summary: Closed H-30.1 (Combine was a Phase-1 not_implemented stub even though walking-skeleton milestone #6 + Combine modal + help-modal + user-guide all claimed it worked). Shipped a pure pdf-lib combine engine (src/main/pdf-ops/combine.ts), a DI handler (src/ipc/handlers/pdf-combine.ts), a path-only file picker channel (dialog:pickPdfFiles with multi: true), and 24 new tests. Riley's parallel commits (1ca9a82 + f7d28d6 + 277f7a3) wired the modal end-to-end against my contracts.ts edits in the same wave.
patterns: JS-strip is automatic-by-construction via pdf-lib's copyPages — copyPages copies the page tree only; document-level /Names → /JavaScript / /OpenAction / /AcroForm / /Outlines live at the source catalog and never get carried into a PDFDocument.create()-rooted output. Verify with a structural-catalog assertion on the OUTPUT (load + catalog.get(PDFName.of('Names')) === undefined), NOT a substring search on the bytes (which fails when useObjectStreams: true compresses the marker strings). For the SOURCE-side sanity check in tests, save the JS-laden fixture with useObjectStreams: false so the marker IS visible as plain text in the buffer.

pdf-lib's PDFDocument.create() with no addPage() saves to a doc whose /Pages tree is empty, but the load() roundtrip materializes a SINGLE default page (page count = 1). So the engine's 0-page skip branch is dead-in-practice for pdf-lib-authored inputs (defensible for real-world PDFs with /Pages.kids=[]). Pin the observed roundtrip behavior with an explicit test so a future pdf-lib upgrade that changes the empty-doc semantics surfaces here.

File-picker channel: chose Option B (new dialog:pickPdfFiles) over Option A (extending dialog:openPdf with multi: true). openPdf is canonical 'open + read + register'; overloading with a paths-only response shape breaks the discriminated-response invariant. Combine doesn't pre-read N files — pdf:combine reads them itself with per-source error mapping. Pattern reusable for future scan-then-combine + batch-import flows.

Schema-first parallel-wave discipline works as documented: ship contracts.ts edits FIRST (new DialogPickPdfFilesRequest|Response + extended PdfCombineError + extended PdfCombineValue), then engine + handler + tests. Riley's parallel renderer wave landed mid-flight (committed 1ca9a82 + f7d28d6 + 277f7a3 to main while I was running tests); my typecheck/test came up clean across the composed state with zero rework. The discriminated-Result + zod-boundary + DI-shape pattern from earlier waves scales to live parallel collaboration when the contract surface ships first.
evidence: Three typechecks clean (main, preload, renderer). vitest run src/main src/ipc src/preload → 873/873 PASS. eslint clean across all touched files. 'Phase 1 stub' / 'Wave 2 follow-up' / not_implemented strings GONE from any pdf:combine path. Commit d5bbab8 pushed to origin/main.
outcome_detail: H-30.1 fully closed. M-30.1 partially addressed: kept the pdf:getOutline honest-stub (zero renderer callers) and rewrote the stale 'Wave 2 follow-up' comment to honest dead-code status. Cannot delete the type entirely without touching src/client/services/api.ts (Riley-owned); documented the future-wave cross-process change.

task: v0.7.6 cut + L-002 visual verification of polish + audit + Combine-fix release
detail: Released v0.7.6 cleanly via CI: bump 0.7.5->0.7.6, husky pre-push gates (lint+typecheck) green, tag pushed -> release.yml run 26754179553 succeeded in 3m50s on windows-2025-vs2026 with Node 20 + native rebuild + electron-builder --publish always producing a draft GitHub Release with 4 assets, promoted to Latest via gh release edit --draft=false. L-002 visual verification on the packaged binary covered all six v0.7.6 user-visible deltas (crisp render / zoom-to-cursor 100% -> 133% / 13-section Help modal / Combine PDFs H-30.1 end-to-end / About v0.7.6 / icon regression) with zero pageerror across the full run. Non-obvious takeaway #1: Playwright's page.mouse.wheel(0,-dy) combined with keyboard.down('Control') does NOT set ctrlKey:true on the resulting WheelEvent under Electron's Chromium build — the renderer's `if (!e.ctrlKey && !e.metaKey) return` zoom-gate at pdf-viewer/index.tsx:130 skips the handler entirely. Workaround: dispatch a native `new WheelEvent('wheel', { ctrlKey:true, deltaY:-120, deltaMode:0, clientX, clientY, bubbles:true, cancelable:true })` directly against the scroller element (selected via `[role="region"][aria-label="Document viewer"]`). This is exactly the event shape a real user's ctrl+wheel produces. Logged for global propagation — every future L-002 packaging wave verifying zoom-to-cursor or other ctrl+modifier wheel behavior should follow this pattern. Non-obvious takeaway #2: when Marcus's brief says doc-pass commits are 'on main' but they aren't (Nathan's README/api-ref/dev-guide/user-guide edits showed as unstaged at wave start), per file-ownership rules the cross-roster commit boundary holds firm — stash them with a self-describing label (`nathan-docs-pending-v076-not-mine-to-commit`) and surface the gap clearly in the build-report + summary. Don't silently commit another agent's files even if the surrounding work is otherwise blocking. Non-obvious takeaway #3: the Combine modal click sequence wanted File menu, not Tools — the H-30.1 closure put Combine PDFs... at the END of the File menu (line 156, after the export-block divider) not under Tools as I initially assumed. Always grep the menu structure for the verb before scripting menu-bar nav.
fix: For future polish-wave L-002 verifiers: (1) use direct WheelEvent dispatch with ctrlKey:true for any ctrl+modifier wheel test under Electron+Playwright; (2) verify file-ownership before staging cross-roster files even when their inclusion would otherwise complete the release; (3) grep the menu-bar structure for the verb's parent menu before scripting `page.locator('button', { hasText: 'Tools' }).click()`-style navigation.

summary: Promoted Diego's stashed v0.7.5→v0.7.6 docs pass (README + api-reference + developer-guide + user-guide) to main via commit e63d459. Bumped version stamps and wove the three 0.7.6 headline deltas (cursor-anchored Ctrl+wheel zoom; 13-section in-app Help modal; H-30.1 Combine-PDFs end-to-end closure) into prose. Husky pre-commit (lint-staged + prettier on markdown) and pre-push (full typecheck + lint --max-warnings 0) both gated green. Live release docs at https://github.com/SuperiorAg/PDF_Viewer_Editor now match v0.7.6 reality.
key_lessons: Cross-roster-stash hand-off pattern is a real coordination primitive. When agent A (Diego, packaging) finds uncommitted edits in agent B's (Nathan, docs) owned files at release-cut time, the right move is: stash with a self-describing label, surface the gap in build-report gaps+handoffs, let the orchestrator dispatch B with the stash hash. Three competing concerns reconciled: release atomicity + work preservation + file-ownership boundary. The label MUST be self-describing because the next agent finds it via 'git stash list' — opaque labels like 'stash@{0}: WIP' force a read of the full diff to figure out scope; 'nathan-docs-pending-v076-not-mine-to-commit' tells the whole story in the listing.

Stashed-doc-promotion has a quiet failure mode: the stash represents a docs delta against a specific code state, and by the time it pops, the code may have moved. In this case the docs claimed 0.7.5; the live binary is 0.7.6; the gap I had to close was wider than the stash diff. Two-step move: pop the stash to recover the work, THEN add version-bump-and-headline-delta edits on top. Reviewers should grep for the OLD version (0.7.5) after the pop to be sure nothing got stamped at the wrong release.

Lint-staged + prettier-on-markdown will reformat tables on commit — that's not a problem, but be aware the committed file may differ in whitespace/alignment from what you wrote in Edit. The husky hook is the gate that ensures the final committed prose conforms to the project's markdown style; trust it and don't fight it by hand-formatting tables to columns. (System reminder confirmed README.md was reformatted by lint-staged at commit time; commit went through cleanly with e63d459.)

Honesty discipline check: when bumping 'as of 0.7.X' prose, ALSO bump the 'phase status' banner at the top of api-reference.md (line 5) and the 'channel status (read this)' banner near the Phase 7 section (line 1490). Those two are the load-bearing 'current as of' stamps users grep for; if you bump version 1 but not the other, a careful reader catches the inconsistency. Both bumped here.

task: Bug fix: Settings -> General 'Make default' surfaced Phase-1 not_implemented to user
summary: Replaced the honest-stub fail('not_implemented') in app:setDefaultPdfHandler with a shell.openExternal('ms-settings:defaultapps?registeredAppName=...') that navigates the user to the Windows OS UI. Same pattern Edge / Adobe Reader / Chrome use — modern Windows 10/11 won't let an app silently flip the default file association; the OS gates it. Gated on process.platform === 'win32' (non-Windows returns false + falls back to honest not_implemented). Kept getDefaultPdfHandlerStatus as not_implemented (deprecated IApplicationAssociationRegistration is the only reliable read path; we don't want that dep). Kept AppSetDefaultPdfHandlerRequest.enable on the contract for stability + Riley follow-up; handler ignores it now.
patterns: The 'honest stub' Phase-1 pattern (return not_implemented + surface to user) is right when the underlying mechanism truly doesn't exist, but wrong when the right mechanism is a navigation to OS UI. Three years of Windows UX research went into making ms-settings:defaultapps the gate — accepting that gate is BOTH more honest than a Phase-1 stub AND closes the user-visible bug. When you see a not_implemented stub being surfaced, the question is not 'can we implement the silent path' (often no, by OS design) but 'is there a user-confirmable OS path we should hand the user off to'.

On the Result<,> ok-path for a handoff-to-OS-UI flow: return BOTH a low-information honest signal (isNowDefault: false — we don't know yet) AND a UX-signaling tag (prompt: 'shown') so the renderer can pick the right copy. Don't lie (claim true) and don't drop info (omit the prompt signal). The renderer can then show 'you have been redirected; confirm there' instead of 'success — now default'.

Test-side pattern for a previously-stub handler: replace the single 'stub returns not_implemented' case with TWO cases — the new ok-path AND the still-failing fallback path. Don't just flip the one test green; keep the not_implemented branch covered as the explicit non-Windows / shell-failed contract.
evidence: vitest src/ipc/handlers/app.test.ts 8/8 pass (was 7 before; +1 added, 1 updated). vitest src/ipc/handlers 47 files / 344 tests green. tsc main+preload+renderer clean. eslint --max-warnings 0 clean. Husky pre-commit (lint-staged + main-tsc) gated green. Husky pre-push (full lint + 3-tsconfig typecheck) gated green. Commit 47ccb70 pushed to origin/main.
outcome_detail: Backend scope fully closed. Renderer follow-up dispatched cleanly to Riley: (1) Settings modal toast copy in src/client/components/modals/settings-modal/index.tsx — replace the now-misleading 'now default' / 'no longer default' success strings with a 'redirected to Windows Settings' string keyed off res.value.prompt === 'shown'; (2) en-US + es-ES i18n keys for the new copy; (3) settings-phase7.test.tsx — update the success-path expectations.

task: Renderer follow-up to commit 47ccb70: honest UX for Settings -> Files -> Make default
summary: Replaced toggleDefaultHandler with openDefaultHandlerSettings; dropped the toggle UX and the derived Currently-default status (we genuinely cannot read it on modern Windows). New copy: button "Open Windows Default apps settings"; status sentence "Status is set in Windows Settings -> Default apps. We don't read it from here."; success banner "Opened Windows Settings -> Default apps. Choose PDF_Viewer_Editor there to confirm."; honest fallback "Runtime default-app changes aren't supported on this OS. Set the default manually in your OS settings." Same four keys translated to es-ES (natural Spanish, no English placeholders). settings-phase7.test.tsx: flipped getDefaultPdfHandlerStatus mock to fail(not_implemented) and added 3 new tests asserting (1) no derived-status leak, (2) success-path redirect banner, (3) not_implemented fallback toast.
patterns: When an IPC handler returns a low-information ok-path (e.g. isNowDefault always false + prompt:"shown") the renderer MUST key its copy off the UX-signaling tag (prompt) not the lying value (isNowDefault). Driving the toast off a value the backend explicitly cannot know is the classic trust-floor violation — it produces a banner that is wrong roughly half the time. The fix is mechanical once you see it: drop the read of the unknowable field, surface the OS-handoff fact instead. Generalizes to any UI atop a hand-off-to-OS-UI flow (signing dialogs, default-app pickers, OAuth consent prompts).

When you delete a derived-status read because the underlying source went honestly not_implemented, also delete the form-state field that backed it (here: isDefaultHandler: boolean | null). Keeping a dead null-forever field in state is a foot-gun — a future Wave-N renderer agent grep will hit it and reason as if it were still load-bearing. Preserve the IPC call as void api.app.getDefaultPdfHandlerStatus() so the channel stays exercised + the preload surface stays referenced from the renderer (otherwise tree-shaking and IPC-channel registry warnings creep in).

i18n key sweep before deletion: grep the entire src/ tree for the OLD key names before removing them from the locale JSONs. The settings.json files are shared with NO TypeScript compile-time enforcement against missing keys, so a dangling t("settings:files.nowDefault") in code would render as the literal key string at runtime — invisible to tsc + lint but visible to the user. Sweep first; in this case grep -r "makeDefault|relinquishDefault|nowDefault|noLongerDefault" only hit settings-modal/index.tsx (which I rewrote) + docs/build-report.md (which is intentionally historical).
evidence: vitest src/client/components/modals/settings-modal/ 2 files / 15 tests green (was 12; +3 new). tsc main+preload+renderer all clean. eslint --max-warnings 0 clean across all touched files. Diego can now safely cut v0.7.7 with the full end-to-end Make-Default fix landed.

task: v0.7.7 release cut + L-002 visual verification (PDF_Viewer_Editor — Make Default fix)
detail: Cut v0.7.7 cleanly: lint 0, typecheck clean (3 tsconfigs), vitest 1844/1844 green after L-003 Node-ABI rebuild, CI release.yml run 26762742191 success in 3m26s on windows-2025-vs2026, draft promoted with 4 assets (latest.yml + NSIS + portable + blockmap), live release at https://github.com/SuperiorAg/PDF_Viewer_Editor/releases/tag/v0.7.7 marked Latest. L-002 visual verification: in-app shots via Playwright _electron + OS-level Windows Settings capture via PowerShell GDI (System.Drawing.Bitmap + Graphics.CopyFromScreen against PrimaryScreen.Bounds). Three durable lessons worth carrying forward: (1) PLAYWRIGHT-ELECTRON SINGLETON CLEANUP: between successive _electron.launch({args:['.']}) runs, leftover helper processes from a failed earlier run leave the singleton lock held and subsequent launches exit cleanly with code=0 and 'Process failed to launch!' — no stderr. Fix: kill all *PDF_Viewer* path processes and remove %APPDATA%/pdf-viewer-editor/{SingletonLock,SingletonCookie,SingletonSocket} before each fresh launch. Burned ~10 min before diagnosing because the symptom looks like the dist/main bundle is broken, but it's not. (2) GDI CAPTURE IS A LEGIT L-002 PATH when the desktop-operator MCP isn't in the agent's tool surface — System.Drawing.Bitmap + Graphics.CopyFromScreen against [System.Windows.Forms.Screen]::PrimaryScreen.Bounds produces a valid PNG of the entire desktop including OS-owned windows (Windows Settings in this case), which is exactly what's needed to prove that shell.openExternal('ms-settings:defaultapps?registeredAppName=...') actually invoked the OS Settings UI in response to an in-app click. The MCP gap is real (Riley hit it in Wave 28a a11y) — the GDI fallback should be the documented L-002 path 2 in the playbook. (3) WINDOW HWND COORDS GO STALE AFTER ShowWindow(SW_MAXIMIZE) — a captured screenshot's pixel coords don't map 1:1 to screen coords after a window maximize because the rect changes; clicking from memorized image coords without re-querying GetWindowRect lands outside the window and either dismisses the modal or closes the app. The repeatable pattern is: maximize, sleep 600ms, GetWindowRect, capture, only then click — or skip the manual click path entirely and use Playwright DOM selectors (button:has-text('Open Windows Default apps settings').click()), which is what worked end-to-end. Bonus observation: the v0.7.7 unpacked binary's process name is 'electron' (not 'PDF Viewer & Editor') because electron-builder --win --dir copies the dev binary as-is; the NSIS installer flow does the rename. Doesn't affect any user-visible behavior — the window title is correctly 'PDF_Viewer_Editor' and the About tab reads 0.7.7.

task: v0.7.7 hotfix — remove duplicate native Electron menu (PDF_Viewer_Editor)
detail: User screenshot of installed v0.7.7 showed two stacked menu bars: Electron's default native menu (File/Edit/View/Window/Help) above Riley's custom React MenuBar (File/Edit/Insert/View/Tools/Help). Root cause: src/main/window-manager.ts never set autoHideMenuBar and src/main/index.ts never called Menu.setApplicationMenu(null), so Electron's built-in app menu auto-attached to every BrowserWindow. Fix is belt-and-suspenders: (a) autoHideMenuBar:true in BrowserWindowConstructorOptions prevents Alt from revealing a hidden default, (b) Menu.setApplicationMenu(null) at top of app.whenReady().then(...) removes the default entirely so no window inherits it. Non-obvious takeaway: Electron's default Menu is non-null on app boot — any Electron app with a custom React/HTML menu MUST explicitly suppress it via setApplicationMenu(null) AND set autoHideMenuBar:true on every BrowserWindow. Neither flag is the Electron default. Just one (autoHideMenuBar alone) is insufficient on Windows because Alt can still toggle the hidden default into view; just the other (setApplicationMenu(null) alone) is sufficient but the autoHideMenuBar belt protects against a future regression that re-installs a Menu somewhere. Test wiring takeaway: src/main/index.ts has no prior test coverage because it self-executes bootstrap() on import — to unit-test it I had to mock the full electron surface + every transitive collaborator (db, ipc, telemetry, auto-update, export, ocr, scan, csp, window-manager) so the bootstrap can run without a real Electron runtime. The microtask flush (two awaited Promise.resolve()) is required so the .then() handler attached to the immediately-resolved whenReady() actually runs before the assertion. 532 main-process tests green, lint+typecheck clean.

task: v0.7.8 hotfix — Forms-area WCAG-AA contrast (5-file dark-token-island sweep)
detail: User-reported UX bug on freshly-installed v0.7.7/0.7.8: 'forms menu has dark buttons that you cannot read unless you click on it'. Root cause was a five-file dark-token island in the forms surface (forms-panel, form-designer, form-fill-overlay, save-template-modal, mail-merge-modal) authored against a fictional --surface-0/--text-1/--border-1/--accent-700/--danger-400 namespace that does not exist anywhere in src/client/styles/tokens.css. Every var() call fell through to its hardcoded dark hex fallback. Most-visible failure: mail-merge-modal and save-template-modal buttons set background:var(--surface-0,#131316) + color:inherit; the light modal-shell cascades --color-text-primary #1f2024 from <body>; result was #1f2024 text on #131316 background (contrast ~1.05:1) — illegible at rest, only revealed by :hover which overrode either color or background. Exactly matches the user-reported symptom. Sweep grep 'rg var(--(surface-[0-9]|text-[12]|border-1|accent-[0-9]{3}|danger-[0-9]{3}|warning-[0-9]{3}))' confirmed the island was confined to those five files; every other component already used --color-* tokens.
fix: Rewrote all five module.css files to route every color/background/border through the real tokens in src/client/styles/tokens.css (--color-bg-surface, --color-text-primary, --color-border-strong, --color-accent-blue, --color-text-inverse, etc.). Normalised default/hover/focus-visible/disabled states across all five files. Forms-panel commit/discard/template/designer/save buttons now light-bg-dark-text ~16:1 contrast; commitBanner now accent-blue with text-inverse ~5.5:1; mail-merge-modal back/cancel buttons now light-bg-dark-text ~16:1 (was ~1.05:1); nextButton primary CTA accent-blue+text-inverse ~5.5:1; save-template input light-bg-dark-text ~16:1 with focus-ring; form-fill-overlay text/dropdown inputs use --color-text-primary on the yellow form-overlay background ~14:1. Final grep confirms zero remaining off-namespace var() references in src/client/.
lesson: When fixing a button-color bug, sweep ALL variants (default + hover + focus + active + disabled) AND ALL sibling files that share the same off-namespace token. Half-fixing default while hover stays correct produces the 'only readable when you click on it' symptom and ships to users. Equally important: dark-token islands are silent — `var(--name, fallback-hex)` falls through to the fallback with no warning, no lint error, no console message. If the fallback was authored for a dark theme but the app ships light, the result is dark-on-dark invisibility indistinguishable from a typo. Audit pattern that catches it: rg 'var(--' src/client/ | rg -v -- '--(color-|space-|font-|radius-|shadow-|z-|focus-|...known-namespaces)'. Worth wiring into a lint rule in a future Phase-7-style sweep.
verification: lint 0 warnings; typecheck (3 tsconfigs) clean; targeted vitest 4 files / 28 tests pass; full client vitest 67 files / 610 tests pass; grep regression for off-namespace tokens returns 0 matches. Visual verification at the running app deferred to Diego at v0.7.9 cut per the brief's L-002 deferral clause (the electron-vite dev server hit an unrelated ESM-loader BrowserWindow import crash today; the renderer Vite build itself succeeded — the bug was pure CSS, no markup/code change, and the token palette is centrally defined so the change is provably correct from static analysis).

Version bump f1f0295, tag v0.7.9, CI run 26768409308 GREEN in 3m51s on windows-2025-vs2026 / Node 20 / electron-builder --publish always. 4 assets uploaded (latest.yml + portable .exe + setup .exe + blockmap). Promoted draft to Latest. Pre-flight: lint 0/0, typecheck clean across 3 tsconfigs, vitest 1846/1846 in 20.34s. L-002 visual verification via Playwright _electron driver against locally repackaged win-unpacked (CI artifacts identical from same tag SHA). Measured WCAG contrast in-renderer (relative-luminance + bg-chain walk for transparency): Forms-panel buttons 16.27:1 at rest (rgb(31,32,36) text on rgb(255,255,255) bg) vs ~1.05:1 dark-on-dark before the fix — measurably correct. 5 screenshots captured: forms-tab-readable, mail-merge-modal, form-designer-palette (Text/Checkbox/Radio/Dropdown/Signature/Date/Select/Exit all readable), only-one-menu-bar (v0.7.8 fix holds, customMenuCount=1), about-v079 (versionHit=1). pageerror=0. Network was stable this cut (prior v0.7.8 had socket error mid-run).
reusable: For a11y-hotfix releases, script WCAG contrast computation in-renderer (getComputedStyle + walk bg chain to first non-transparent ancestor + WCAG 2.x relative-luminance formula) to produce measurable, citeable contrast ratios — beats 'looks readable in the screenshot' qualitatively. Pattern lives in scripts/wave-v079-l002-verify.mjs. On flaky network, RETRY git/gh commands rather than wait — never amend/force-push if a step ambiguously succeeded; verify remote state first via `gh release view` and `git log origin/main`.

task: v0.7.9+ hotfix — click-to-place default-sized form fields (Phase 3.1)
detail: User-reported UX bug: 'Nothing happens when trying to add a text field.' Root cause src/client/components/form-designer/index.tsx:135-138 — onMouseUp required screenWidth>=4 && screenHeight>=4 to dispatch designAddFieldThunk; a pure click (or <4px drag) returned early with no feedback. The 'Phase 3.1 may seed a default-sized rect' comment was an unfinished stub from the original Phase 3 wave. Shipped Phase 3.1: on click below CLICK_THRESHOLD_PX (4) on either axis, seed a default-sized rect at the click point. Defaults in PDF user-space points: text 144x18, checkbox 14x14, radio 14x14, dropdown 144x18, signature 180x36, date 100x18. Extracted geometry into pure computePlacementPdfRect() helper for unit-test surface without mounting IPC+thunk stack. Cursor: added .canvasOverlaySelect modifier so Select tool keeps cursor:default while placement tools get cursor:crosshair (already in CSS). 13 tests green (6 new: text/checkbox/signature defaults, threshold boundary, zoom semantics, type-completeness sentinel). Lint 0 on my files, full typecheck clean.
lesson: DRAG-ONLY WITH NO CLICK FALLBACK is a recurring UX anti-pattern. When a tool requires drag>=N px and offers no click path, users with imprecise mouse control, touchpads, or who instinctively click before discovering 'you have to drag' will report 'nothing happens.' Always offer a sensible default for the click path — this is what Photoshop/Illustrator/Figma all do. The 'Phase X.1 may seed a default-sized rect' style of TODO comment is a tell: if you ship a drag-only interaction with a stub for the click path, ship the click path with it, not in a 'maybe later' phase. The 4px threshold itself is fine — users absolutely produce sub-4px drags by accident — but the threshold should branch INTO a default, not OUT of the dispatch.
fix: For any drag-to-create-rect interaction in this codebase: (a) extract the screen->target-space rect math into a pure helper that takes {drag:{start,current}, zoom, pageHeight, type} -> {x,y,width,height}, (b) inside the helper, branch on |drag| < threshold and return defaults[type] seeded at drag.start, (c) test the threshold boundary, zoom semantics (defaults are in target-space units NOT screen px), and exhaustive type coverage in unit tests. Sibling instance flagged at src/client/components/annotation-layer/index.tsx:157 (same width<4||height<4 pattern) — out of scope this commit, reported back.
verification: vitest 13/13 in form-designer; eslint 0 warnings on changed files; main typecheck clean (husky pre-commit); full 3-tsconfig typecheck + full lint clean (husky pre-push); pushed to origin/main as 348a225. Visual verification of running binary deferred to next Diego packaging wave per L-002 deferral pattern (v0.7.8 precedent) — change is provably correct from static analysis (pure geometry helper unit-tested + single-line CSS modifier).
cross_wave_friction: David's parallel OCR wave had uncommitted WIP referencing AppDiagnoseOcr* contract + diagnoseOcr AppDeps method that broke the pre-push typecheck. Mitigated by stashing 4 named WIP stashes (david-wip-ipc-contract / david-wip-preload / david-ocr-wip-app-handler / david-ocr-wip-temp-for-riley-push), pushing my commit, then restoring all stashes. One residual stash (david-wip-ocr-engine-residual) preserved for David to inspect. Takeaway for parallel-wave coordination: when David needs to add types to a Riley-owned file (src/client/types/ipc-contract.ts), the cleaner pattern is a small Riley-owned commit that adds the type re-exports first so David's WIP doesn't cross the file-ownership line.

lesson: User-facing toast read 'rasterize page 0 failed: Error' because four sites in src/main/pdf-ops/ocr-engine.ts (and 13 other sites across pdf-ops + ocr-* handlers + language-pack-manager + wia-scanner) used `(e as Error).name ?? 'unknown'` instead of `.message`. `Error.name` defaults to the constructor name ('Error', 'TypeError', etc.) for every `throw new Error(msg)` — it conveys NOTHING about the failure. The pattern was copy-pasted across the entire OCR subsystem. Worse: the dev test path mocked rasterizePage so the engine never actually saw a real packaging-layer canvas failure — `engine.test.ts:397` asserted r.error==='pdf_render_failed' but never inspected r.message, so the 'Error' suffix was invisible to CI.
fix: Replace every `(e as Error).name ?? 'unknown'` with `safeMessage(e, 'unknown error')` from src/shared/result.ts (Julian's 2026-06-01 production-safe helper — returns `.message` in dev/test, generic fallback in prod). Update the regression test to assert r.message contains the underlying error string ('synthetic-raster-fail'), not just the discriminant. Also hardened the canvas-load failure path in ocr-bootstrap.ts: ERR_DLOPEN_FAILED now produces an explicit 'packaging issue, not config' message that suggests reinstall, distinguishing it from the truly-missing-module case. Added app:diagnoseOcr IPC channel (devtools-only, no UI yet) that reports canvasModuleResolvable / canvasModuleLoadError / pdfjsLoadable / tesseractCoreReachable / documentStoreCount so the next user report can be triaged in one paste-back. Pattern is universal — any error rendered into a user-facing string MUST use .message (or safeMessage); .name is for programmatic error-type checks (e.g. 'AbortError') only.
recurrence: first occurrence — pattern is now in the .name-vs-.message hard-won lesson for all future agents on this repo.

task: v0.7.12 OCR canvas polyfill + createNodeHttpsStreamer hardening (recovery commit)
detail: Three lessons. (1) Agent-run zombification: a subagent's main process can die without delivering a completion notification or pushing its uncommitted edits. The working-tree diff is the truth; the agent's reasoning is recoverable from comments + structure of the edits left behind. Recovery pattern: trust the working-tree diff; trace the prior agent's intent from inline comments and the test fixtures left in place; commit + push their work attributed via Co-Authored-By in the commit body (e.g. 'David (recovered run)'). Do NOT discard or rewrite their edits unless they are demonstrably wrong. (2) Audit-driven critical fixes that touch the SAME file as in-flight work belong in the same next commit, not a separate follow-up. LOCK 0036 (parallel-write corruption) makes file-level batching the safe move. Both `createNodeHttpsStreamer` error-handler fixes shipped in the polyfill commit because all three edits were in `src/main/pdf-ops/ocr-bootstrap.ts`. (3) Mocking response-stream error paths via vi.mock + EventEmitter stubs requires double `queueMicrotask`: one to defer the response callback invocation past the streamer's `httpsGet` return, another to defer the error emit past the response callback's listener wiring. Synchronous emits from inside `mockImplementation` fire before the streamer has wired its listener, so the test passes for the wrong reason (no-listener emit = no-op, not rejection).

task: v0.7.12 release cut (OCR rasterize fix via @napi-rs/canvas globalThis polyfill + 2 createNodeHttpsStreamer main-crash guards)
detail: Single-delta release on v0.7.11. Pre-flight: lint 0/0, 3-tsconfig typecheck clean, vitest 1874/1874 (174 files, 20.94s) after Node-ABI rebuild incl. new prod-render @napi-rs/canvas integration + 4 streamer crash regressions. Bump 0.7.11->0.7.12 (9a26cd9), tag v0.7.12 -> release.yml run 26837571178 GREEN in 3m28s on windows-2025-vs2026 + Node 20 + electron-builder --publish always. 4 assets (latest.yml + setup.exe + portable.exe + blockmap), promoted draft->Latest via gh release edit. Structural smoke on local --dir repack (same tag SHA): 4-process Electron family + MainWindowHandle=990730 title=PDF_Viewer_Editor + clean shutdown. L-002 visual SKIPPED (operator/playwright MCPs unbridged this session, matching v0.7.11 posture).
takeaways: For a structural smoke during a release wave, prefer the win-unpacked tree (rebuilt locally via 'electron-builder --win --dir' from the SAME tag SHA) over the published portable .exe. The portable self-extracts to %TEMP% and runs under a different process name than the bootstrap wrapper, breaking Get-Process name-filter checks at T+8-18s and giving a false 'family=1' / 'launch failed' reading. win-unpacked has product-named processes ('PDF Viewer & Editor') matching what users install via NSIS; the CI binary is still the authoritative shipped artifact, the local repack is just to make the smoke filter reliable.

When the brief cites a partial test count ('~388/388' for the OCR + export subset) but the actual full vitest run is much larger (1874/1874), report BOTH the brief number AND the full suite count in build-report. The brief number is correct for its scope; the full count is the load-bearing 'no regression' evidence. Disagreement between them otherwise reads like a counting bug.

For OCR-pipeline bug fixes with no renderer-side touch, the L-002 visual deferral is well-defensible: L-002 was written to catch renderer-broken defects (white screen, preload mismatch, IPC drift), not backend-pipeline correctness. The structural smoke + integration test against the REAL @napi-rs/canvas binding (PNG magic bytes verified) is the lab-grade analogue of a launch screenshot, scoped to the seam that actually changed. Document the rationale per release so the lock stays sharp and the defer doesn't drift into 'always skipped'.

task: Rasterize-failure diagnostic capture (post v0.7.12 OCR polyfill fix)
lesson: When a bug needs the user's data to reproduce, bake the diagnostic INTO the app — do not ask the user to run an external script. App-side log capture + a known log path surfaced in the error toast turns every user report into a self-service bug report. v0.7.10 → v0.7.11 → v0.7.12 each shipped a guess based on synthetic repro of the 'rasterize page 0 failed: Value is none of these types String, Path' error; v0.7.13 ships an instrument that delivers the real native stack the first time a user hits it on their actual PDF.
detail: rasterizePageProd now wraps page.render().promise in a try/catch that builds a full diagnostic record (timestamp, error.{name,message,stack,code,cause}, pdfBytes_length, pageIndex, dpi, scale, canvasWidth/Height, canvas_module + version from package.json, node/electron versions, platform/arch, presence of globalThis.{Image,Path2D,ImageData,DOMMatrix,DOMPoint}) and writes it to userData/logs/ocr-rasterize-<epochMs>.json. Rethrown Error message embeds the absolute log path so the engine safeMessage wrap surfaces it to the toast (NODE_ENV is unset in Electron packaged main, so safeMessage returns the real message). Logging failure NEVER masks the original bug (writeDiagnosticLog returns null + console.error on writeFileSync throw OR app.getPath throw). tryLoadCanvas SUCCESS branch fires a one-time canvas-load.json snapshot guarded by a module-scope boolean. Side lesson: vi.mock('electron') only intercepts ESM imports, NOT CJS require() from inside the module under test. Initial test run failed 'Cannot read properties of undefined (reading getPath)' because the diagnostic helper used a lazy CJS require. Switched to the top-level ESM app binding and tests went green. Generalizable rule: in Electron main-process modules that need to be testable, prefer the top-level ESM import to a defensive lazy require.

task: v0.7.13 - shell-launched PDF handoff fix (Windows Explorer double-click, second-instance argv, macOS open-file)
summary: Fixed the v0.7.12 regression where double-clicking a .pdf in Explorer launched PDF_Viewer_Editor.exe but the document never opened. Root cause: app.on('second-instance', ...) discarded its argv parameter AND process.argv was never inspected on cold-start. Added src/main/argv-parser.ts (parseShellPdfPath, sanitized via existing path-sanitizer + existsSync gate), wired three entry points in src/main/index.ts (cold-start argv -> pendingShellPdf -> dispatch on did-finish-load; warm-start second-instance argv -> dispatch to live window; macOS app.on('open-file') -> dispatch immediately or stash for did-finish-load), new file:openFromShell IPC channel + FileOpenFromShellEvent contract (emit-only main->renderer), preload listener via the existing on/removeListener pattern, bridge-unavailable fallback in services/api.ts.
patterns: single-instance-lock argv MUST be parsed - second-instance handler signature is (_event, argv, _cwd) and discarding argv silently breaks every file-association double-click after the first launch. Same trap on cold-start process.argv. Both paths must run the SAME sanitized parser so the renderer's open-flow stays single-codepath.

macOS app.on('open-file') MUST call e.preventDefault() AND tolerate pre-window-ready firing - Finder pre-resolves the path and fires before app.whenReady() resolves on a cold launch. Stash to a module-scope pendingShellPdf slot for the cold-start dispatch path; only call webContents.send when getMainWindow() is truthy AND webContents.isLoading() is false.

did-finish-load is the correct rendezvous for cold-start shell-handoff - dispatching at app.whenReady() races the renderer's React-mount useEffect that subscribes to the channel. The renderer wires its subscription inside the first useEffect after mount, which fires AFTER the load events. did-finish-load is the documented signal that the page + its scripts are fully loaded.

Renderer-side subscription belongs in app.tsx useEffect (with proper unsubscribe via cleanup), NOT in services/api.ts at module load. Backed out my own module-load approach the moment I saw a parallel agent had placed it correctly. The lifecycle-tied placement avoids module-import-side-effect smell AND avoids the dynamic-import-to-dodge-circular-dep gymnastics.

ALWAYS git stash + re-run pre-existing failing tests to confirm whether failures are caused by your change. Two OCR-bootstrap tests failed under my full src/main run but passed at the stashed baseline - they belonged to an in-flight parallel agent's uncommitted ocr-bootstrap.ts/.test.ts work. Without the stash check I might have falsely blamed my own change or chased a non-existent root cause.
gotchas: initial git status shown at agent dispatch can be STALE - by the time the agent runs, multiple parallel sub-agents may have modified the working tree. Re-run `git status` before committing to see the true state, and stage by explicit filename per LOCK 0036's parallel-write doctrine to avoid accidentally sweeping another agent's work into your commit.

when a parallel agent has already created the renderer-side hook with the same name/contract you planned, READ what they wrote and align - don't double-implement. They wrote subscribeFileOpenFromShell with runtime feature-detection precisely because the API wasn't yet exposed in PdfApi at their write time; my contract change is what flips their feature-detect from no-op-stub to live-subscription.
evidence: npx eslint src/main/argv-parser.ts src/main/argv-parser.test.ts src/main/index.ts src/ipc/contracts.ts src/preload/index.ts src/client/services/api.ts --max-warnings 0 = clean. npx tsc --noEmit across all 3 tsconfigs = clean. npx vitest run src/main src/ipc src/preload = 356 passed across 49 files (8 new argv-parser specs included). Two ocr-bootstrap test failures are parallel-agent work, not mine - confirmed via git stash baseline.

task: v0.7.13 Settings -> Diagnostics tile + file:openFromShell renderer subscription
summary: Surfaced two backend channels in the renderer: David's app:diagnoseOcr IPC got a new Settings -> Diagnostics tab (Run button + Copy + JSON pre + log folder hint + trust-floor disclaimer); file:openFromShell got a subscribeFileOpenFromShell hook wired at app mount that routes shell-opened paths through openDroppedPathThunk (same flow as drag-drop). +8 tests (5 diagnostics-tab + 3 file-open-from-shell); 637/637 src/client suite green; lint + typecheck clean.
key_learning: When backend lands a diagnostic IPC + a new shell-entry channel, the renderer must also expose them in the UI for users to actually benefit. Surface = adoption. Two cheap UI surfaces (a Settings tab and a useEffect subscription) convert backend instrumentation that only DevTools users see into a self-service support workflow.
sub_learning: Feature-detect at runtime when the producer contract isn't fully landed yet. Defensive cast '(window as { pdfApi?: unknown }).pdfApi' + typeof-function narrowing keeps typecheck green during parallel-coordination race windows; subscriber goes from no-op to live the instant the producer property exists, no client edit needed.
parallel_coordination: Zero merge conflicts with two parallel David runs (ocr-bootstrap.ts +247 lines for log file; contracts+main+preload for file:openFromShell channel). File ownership held — David's only Riley-owned edit (api.ts no-op fallback line) is the contract-satisfying stub for the new canonical PdfApi field, not a UI change.
